User task:

Theorem title: Pinsker's inequality via reduction to the Bernoulli case

Ambient theorem statement:
Pinsker's inequality via reduction to the Bernoulli case.

Let \((X,\mathcal F)\) be a measurable space, and let \(P\) and \(Q\) be probability measures on \((X,\mathcal F)\). Assume \(P\ll Q\), so that the Kullback–Leibler divergence
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P\|Q)
:=
\int_X \log\!\left(\frac{dP}{dQ}\right)\,dP
\]
is well defined in \([0,\infty]\).

Then
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P\|Q)\ge 2\,\|P-Q\|_{\mathrm{TV}}^2,
\]
where
\[
\|P-Q\|_{\mathrm{TV}}:=\sup_{A\in\mathcal F}|P(A)-Q(A)|.
\]

Equivalently, if \(\delta:=\|P-Q\|_{\mathrm{TV}}\), then
\[
D_{\mathrm{KL}}(P\|Q)\ge 2\delta^2.
\]

The key local scalar step is the Bernoulli inequality
\[
p\log\!\left(\frac{p}{q}\right)+(1-p)\log\!\left(\frac{1-p}{1-q}\right)\ge 2(p-q)^2,
\qquad 0<p,q<1,
\]
which appears after collapsing the general measures to a two-point partition.

Target node:

{
  "id": "bernoulli_inequality",
  "title": "Bernoulli inequality for relative entropy",
  "informal_statement": "For \\(0<p,q<1\\),\n\\[\np\\log\\!\\left(\\frac pq\\right) + (1-p)\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{1-p}{1-q}\\right) \\ge 2(p-q)^2.\n\\]",
  "informal_proof_text": "Fix \\(q\\in(0,1)\\), and define for \\(p\\in(0,1)\\)\n\\[\nF_q(p) := p\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{p}{q}\\right) + (1-p)\\log\\!\\left(\\frac{1-p}{1-q}\\right) - 2(p-q)^2.\n\\]\nWe will show that \\(F_q(p)\\ge 0\\) for all \\(p\\in(0,1)\\).\n\nEvaluating at \\(p=q\\) yields \\(F_q(q) = 0\\). Now compute the first derivative with respect to \\(p\\):\n\\[\nF_q'(p) = \\log\\!\\left(\\frac{p(1-q)}{q(1-p)}\\right) - 4(p-q).\n\\]\nEvaluating the first derivative at \\(p=q\\) gives \\(F_q'(q) = 0\\).\n\nDifferentiating again, we find the second derivative:\n\\[\nF_q''(p) = \\frac1p + \\frac{1}{1-p} - 4 = \\frac{1}{p(1-p)} - 4.\n\\]\nFor \\(0<p<1\\), the elementary inequality \\(p(1-p)\\le \\frac14\\) holds, which implies \\(\\frac{1}{p(1-p)}\\ge 4\\). Therefore, \\(F_q''(p)\\ge 0\\) for all \\(p\\in(0,1)\\), meaning \\(F_q\\) is convex on \\((0,1)\\).\n\nSince \\(p=q\\) is a critical point of the convex function \\(F_q\\), it is a global minimizer. Since \\(F_q(q)=0\\), it follows that \\(F_q(p)\\ge 0\\) for all \\(p\\in(0,1)\\), which is exactly the claimed inequality.",
  "formalization_priority": 1,
  "formalization_rationale": "This scalar inequality is the core analytical engine of the proof. It requires only single-variable real calculus and is completely decoupled from measure theory and general KL divergence, making it an excellent, self-contained formal island."
}
